title: Back-translation confidence (round-trip SNOMED linkage)
prose: Estimate per-translation confidence by ROUND-TRIP linkage. Given an EN->KO translation, back-translate the Korean term to English, then run that back-translation through semantic/hybrid search over SNOMED. If it links back to the ORIGINAL SNOMED expression (correct concept ranks top / high similarity), the round trip preserved meaning -> high confidence; if it lands on the wrong concept or low rank -> low confidence. This turns 'does meaning survive the round trip?' into a usable per-item confidence signal to triage terminologist review.

nodes.title: SNOMED terminology — International RF2 (local)
nodes.description: English FSN + synonyms per concept, read from the local International-edition RF2 release (fast access). Both the corpus to index and the ground-truth target.

nodes.title: Korean SNOMED extension (snomedct-kr)
nodes.description: Local RF2; Korean designations linked to concept ids. Gold (concept <-> Korean term) pairs to drive + evaluate the round trip.

nodes.title: Candidate KO translation
nodes.description: The EN->KO translation to be scored.

nodes.title: Build SNOMED index  (function)
nodes.description: Function node: reads the International RF2 source, embeds FSN+synonyms (BGE-M3, reusing QdrantHybridStore), and PRODUCES the index artifact. Params: embedding model, indexing strategy (dense/lexical/hybrid). Run once; reused.

nodes.title: SNOMED index artifact  (DATA / DataObject)
nodes.description: The built, reusable index — DATA, not recomputed each run. A DataObject whose provenance/meta records the build config: RF2 release id, embedding model, strategy, vector dim, concept count. Consumed by lookup as a datasource. Rebuilding with a different embedding model yields a DISTINCT artifact, so an investigation stays reproducible.

nodes.title: Back-translate KO->EN
nodes.description: An LLM back-translates the Korean term to English (ideally several samples for self-consistency).

nodes.title: Index lookup / retrieval  (function)
nodes.description: Consumes the index artifact (as a datasource); queries it with the back-translated English; returns MULTIPLE ranked candidate concepts.

nodes.title: Rerank candidates  (function)
nodes.description: Re-rank the retrieved candidates: cross-encoder vs LLM vs calculated metrics. None exists today.

nodes.title: Closeness / distance measure
nodes.description: How close the chosen concept is to the correct one: rank, embedding-similarity distance, SNOMED hierarchy distance for near-misses.

nodes.title: Round-trip linkage -> confidence
nodes.description: Turn closeness into a per-item confidence: high when the round trip recovers the original concept, low when it lands elsewhere.

notes.body: Open questions: (1) hybrid lexical/vector weighting; (2) score calibration vs terminologist 'needs review' labels; (3) partial credit when the hit is an ancestor/sibling rather than the exact concept; (4) FSN vs synonym matching; (5) cost of back-translation + search per item.

SNOMED semantic index — design (new function node, lives in the PLUGIN repo snomed_translation.functions).

REUSE (already in the plugin): BGE-M3 embedder + QdrantHybridStore (dense COSINE + sparse, RRF fusion) in agent/qdrant_store.py; deterministic content-hashed collection naming + resumable indexing + lookup cache (exemplars.py / index_exemplars.py). The Korean SNOMED extension RF2 is ALREADY a local data source (configs/sources/kr_snomed.json, kind 'snomed_national_extension').

NEW: index SNOMED *concepts* (sctid -> FSN + synonyms) instead of EN->KO pairs (payload {sctid, fsn_en, synonyms_en, hierarchy}); a 'given EN term -> best concept' retrieval stage; a reranker (cross-encoder vs LLM vs calculated — none exists today); a closeness metric = rank + cosine + SNOMED hierarchy distance.

DATA DECISION: English FSN+synonyms come from EITHER the International-edition RF2 (local file) OR the Snowstorm MCP (live, scoped by ECL). KR extension is local already; the English side is the open question.
notes.author: design

notes.body:
Index = a produced ARTIFACT (data), not a pure function. Modelled with existing primitives: a function node (build) -> promote -> DataObject -> datasource (consume).

DataObject meta (the 'config clearly recorded'): {rf2_release_id, embedding_model, strategy (dense|lexical|hybrid), vector_dim, n_concepts, scope_ecl?}. Provenance (flow, node, run_id) comes for free from promote().

Reproducibility: flows are content-hash versioned and runs link to a flow version, so re-running an investigation with a DIFFERENT embedding model = duplicate the flow, change the build node's embedding_model param -> a new, distinct index DataObject; the original (with its model) stays recorded. The build (expensive) runs once; lookups are cheap.
notes.author: design

notes.body:
Agent build workflow + the two walls it hits:
1) Check existing flows: all are translate->evaluate pipelines (datasource/translate/evaluate/score/promote) — NONE adaptable to build-index->backtranslate->lookup->rerank. So: new flow from scratch.
2) Check building blocks (list_functions): only translation-domain nodes exist (translate, evaluate, optimize, score, style_guide). The blocks we need — build_snomed_index, retrieval, reranker, concept-distance — DON'T EXIST.
=> Must CREATE the building blocks first (plugin code: FunctionSpec + runner via the semi_automated_research.functions entry point), THEN compose the flow. Creating a block is code, not config. Sequence: obtain RF2 -> write build_snomed_index (+ retrieval/rerank/distance) -> compose flow -> run investigations.
notes.author: workflow

notes.body: FINDING: two miss types — (a) genuine mistranslations (trichinosis -> model said 'Piloerection' -> wrong concept, correctly low confidence); (b) near-misses to a synonymous-but-different concept ('Sexual status finding'). (b) motivates hierarchy-aware distance / a reranker (partial credit for close concepts).
notes.author: investigation

notes.body: Quantitative results are now TRACKED RUNS under this investigation (see the run ledger) -- each is a flow + git shas + recall@K metric, reproducible -- NOT transcribed here. Qualitative read: back-translation substantially beats direct multilingual lookup, and its near-misses are usually recoverable in the top-K, so a reranker should lift recall@1 toward recall@5/@10; direct-KO gains little from top-K. (The earlier hand-typed numbers were retired -- one was even wrong, a different sample -- which is exactly why results must come from runs.)
notes.author: investigation

notes.body:
**Cross-encoder reranking (BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3) lifts recall@1, but candidate generation is the ceiling.** Two tracked runs under this investigation rerank the bi-encoder's top-10:

- Back-translation -> hybrid retrieve -> rerank: recall@1 **55%** (up from ~48-50% un-reranked), @5 68%, @10 75%.
- Direct KO query -> dense retrieve -> rerank: recall@1 **35%** (up from ~23%), but @10 only **37%** — dense cross-lingual retrieval simply never surfaces the gold for ~63% of terms, so no reranker can recover it.

Takeaway: back-translation stays essential — it's what gets the gold concept into the candidate set (top-10 75% vs 37%). Reranking is a cheap +5-12pt @1 win on top of whichever retrieval feeds it. Numbers live in the linked runs (git-sha provenance), not this note.
notes.author: research-agent

notes.body:
**TranslateGemma-27b underperforms the instruction-prompted general model for terminology back-translation.** A/B under this investigation, same pipeline (KO->EN -> hybrid retrieve -> rerank top-10), only the back-translation model changed:

- gemma4-26b-qat (medical-terminologist system prompt): recall@1 **55%**, @5 68%, @10 75%.
- translategemma-27b (kaitchup AutoRound w4a16, structured translate prompt): recall@1 **47%**, @5 60%, @10 **62%**.

Cause: TranslateGemma is a fluency-optimised general translator and emits *descriptive* phrasings ('Acute leukemia, a disease'; 'Insomnia caused by alcohol') rather than terse controlled-vocabulary terms, so they match SNOMED surface forms less well. The drop is largest at @10 (75%->62%) — i.e. it's the candidate-generation ceiling that falls, which reranking cannot recover. For SNOMED lookup, a terse-term instruction prompt beats a specialised translation model. Numbers live in the linked run.
notes.author: research-agent
